title: How We Beat The Favourite - A Lay Of The Loamshire Hunt Cup
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Aye, squire,ן¿½ said Stevens, ן¿½they back him at evens;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The race is all over, bar shouting, they say;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Clown ought to beat her; Dick Neville is sweeter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than ever, he swears he can win all the way.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A gentleman rider, well, Iן¿½m an outsider,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But if heן¿½s a gent who the mischiefן¿½s a jock?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You swells mostly blunder, Dick rides for the plunder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He rides, too, like thunder, he sits like a rock.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He calls ן¿½hunted fairlyן¿½ a horse that has barely
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Been strippן¿½d for a trot within sight of the hounds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A horse that at Warwick beat Birdlime and Yorick,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And gave Abdelkader at Aintree nine pounds.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They say we have no test to warrant a protest;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dick rides for a lord and stands in with a steward;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The light of their faces they show him, his case is
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Prejudged and his verdict already secured.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But none can outlast her, and few travel faster,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She strides in her work clean away from The Drag;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You hold her and sit her, she couldnן¿½t be fitter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whenever you hit her sheן¿½ll spring like a stag.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And pן¿½rhaps the green jacket, at odds though they back it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ May fall, or thereן¿½s no knowing what may turn up;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mare is quite ready, sit still and ride steady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Keep cool; and I think you may just win the Cup.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dark-brown with tan muzzle, just stripped for the tussle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stood Iseult, arching her neck to the curb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A lean head and fiery, strong quarters and wiry,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A loin rather light, but a shoulder superb.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some parting injunction, bestowed with great unction,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I tried to recall, but forgot like a dunce,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Reginald Murray, full tilt on White Surrey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Came down in a hurry to start us at once.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Keep back in the yellow! Come up on Othello!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hold hard on the chestnut! Turn round on The Drag!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Keep back there on Spartan! Back you, sir, in tartan!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, steady there, easy!ן¿½ and down went the flag.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We started, and Kerr made strong running on Mermaid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through furrows that led to the first stake-and-bound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The crack, half extended, lookן¿½d bloodlike and splendid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Held wide on the right where the headland was sound.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I pulled hard to baffle her rush with the snaffle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before her two-thirds of the field got away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All through the wet pasture where floods of the last year
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still loitered, they clotted my crimson with clay.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fourth fence, a wattle, floorן¿½d Monk and Bluebottle;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Drag came to grief at the blackthorn and ditch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The rails toppled over Redoubt and Red Rover,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lane stopped Lycurgus and Leicestershire Witch.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She passed like an arrow Kildare and Cock Sparrow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Mantrap and Mermaid refused the stone wall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Giles on The Greyling came down at the paling,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I was left sailing in front of them all.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I took them a burster, nor eased her nor nursed her
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until the Black Bullfinch led into the plough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And through the strong bramble we bored with a scramble,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My cap was knockן¿½d off by the hazel-tree bough.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where furrows looked lighter I drew the rein tighter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her dark chest all dappled with flakes of white foam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her flanks mud-bespattered, a weak rail she shattered,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We landed on turf with our heads turnן¿½d for home.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then crashן¿½d a low binder, and then close behind her
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sward to the strokes of the favourite shook;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His rush roused her mettle, yet ever so little
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She shortened her stride as we raced at the brook.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She rose when I hit her. I saw the stream glitter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wide scarlet nostril flashed close to my knee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Between sky and water The Clown came and caught her,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The space that he cleared was a caution to see.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And forcing the running, discarding all cunning,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A length to the front went the rider in green;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A long strip of stubble, and then the big double,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two stiff flights of rails with a quickset between.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She raced at the rasper, I felt my knees grasp her,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I found my hands give to her strain on the bit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She rose when The Clown did, our silks as we bounded
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brushן¿½d lightly, our stirrups clashן¿½d loud as we lit.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A rise steeply sloping, a fence with stone coping,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The last, we diverged round the base of the hill;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His path was the nearer, his leap was the clearer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I floggן¿½d up the straight, and he led sitting still.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She came to his quarter, and on still I brought her,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And up to his girth, to his breastplate she drew;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A short prayer from Neville just reachן¿½d me, ן¿½The devil!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He mutterן¿½d, lockן¿½d level the hurdles we flew.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A hum of hoarse cheering, a dense crowd careering,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All sights seen obscurely, all shouts vaguely heard;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The green wins!ן¿½ ן¿½The crimson!ן¿½ The multitude swims on,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And figures are blended and features are blurrן¿½d.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The horse is her master!ן¿½ ן¿½The green forges past her!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The Clown will outlast her!ן¿½ ן¿½The Clown wins!ן¿½ ן¿½The Clown!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The white railing races with all the white faces,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The chestnut outpaces, outstretches the brown.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On still past the gateway she strains in the straightway,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still struggles, ן¿½The Clown by a short neck at most,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He swerves, the green scourges, the stand rocks and surges,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And flashes, and verges, and flits the white post.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Aye! so ends the tussle, I knew the tan muzzle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was first, though the ring-men were yelling ן¿½Dead heat!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A nose I could swear by, but Clarke said, ן¿½The mare by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A short head.ן¿½ And thatן¿½s how the favourite was beat.
meaning:
"How We Beat The Favourite - A Lay Of The Loamshire Hunt Cup" is a quintessential example of Adam Lindsay Gordon's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
